questions.text: I believe that the moral weight of suffering, once I have truly registered it, cannot be set aside without a cost to my integrity.
I generally dictate the terms of a collaboration.
I feel that if no one leads, nothing meaningful gets accomplished.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is what transforms ethical knowledge into ethical practice.
I find that having a morning routine sets a productive tone for the entire day.
I routinely accept inconvenience so that other people's lives run more smoothly.
I rarely pick up on the nonverbal negotiation that happens before a group makes a decision.
I find that I contribute most when I maintain an objective stance rather than allowing myself to be moved by the emotional gravity of a situation.
I tend to match the pace others have set rather than impose my own.
I routinely optimize my schedule around my own peak performance.
I detect when someone uses humor to deflect from something they find painful.
I believe my capacity for intense feeling is both a gift and a burden.
A messy desk doesn't bother me; I know where everything is in my own way.
I feel that personal accountability is the engine that drives real progress.
I hold the view that most communication happens in what is left unsaid.
I routinely eat last, leave last, and take the smallest portion.
I experience embarrassment as a passing moment rather than a lingering burn.
I often replay emotional moments in my mind and re-experience them at nearly full intensity.
